messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"som-window-semantics-year1-zero","title":"The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month accrual read as an annual snapshot — the pre-launch clock makes even that generous","narrative":"obtainableFactor's 1% was 'corrected' in refinement cycle 1 and now looks conservative and triangulated. But the benchmark corpus it leans on defines SOM as a 1–3 year (Prospeo, IdeaPlan) or 3–5 year (Wise) cumulative capture, not a Year-1 run-rate, and the one worked example in the corpus lands at 0.1% of SAM — 5x below the band's own floor. Layer the venture's actual clock on top: it is pre-launch, so months 1–6 are certification, listing and pipeline-building, not selling. A 'conservative ramp' that books its first PO in month 7–9 doesn't earn 1% of SAM; it earns a fraction of the back half of the year. The band's low edge (0.5%) is not the pessimistic case — it is the optimistic reading of the sources' own semantics.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The Prospeo/IdeaPlan/Wise benchmark family defines the 1–5% obtainable share as accruing over 1–3 or 3–5 years, not per-year; the ledger pins 1% strictly to Year 1","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c1","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c5","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c6","ledger:obtainableFactor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The only concrete worked example in the corpus yields SOM/SAM ≈ 0.1% ($3.2M/$3.2B) for years 1–2, and the Tractian comparable started at $200K — not the ~$1.2M first-year figure the ledger's crossCheck asserts","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c7","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c10"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The venture is pre-launch with a conservative-ramp profile: the selling window inside Year 1 is 6–9 months at best, so any annualized benchmark must be pro-rated before it is applied","refs":["profile:Stage","profile:What obtainable horizon should Year-1 (YAM) assume?"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor at 0.01 overstates the realistic Year-1 capture by 2–3x; the defensible point sits at or below the band floor","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The node was already corrected once (3%→1% in refinement cycle 1) and carries maturity 'triangulated' — it reads as the fixed, conservative number. The residual error is not the value but the time-semantics of the sources it cites, which no band edge encodes.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A comparable early-stage DC-hardware entrant (intelligent PDU or adjacent rack power gear) documented capturing ≥1% of its serviceable market within its first 12 calendar months from launch.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"Three of four benchmark sources in the digest explicitly frame the percentage as multi-year, and the single worked example sits 5x below the band floor; the offsetting uncertainty is that the venture may have pre-launch pipeline the model doesn't credit.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.004,"note":"Pro-rated Year-1 capture consistent with cumulative-window benchmarks and the 0.1% worked example"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Months from launch to first booked CE purchase order","where":"Own CRM plus TED (EU public tender portal) responses to rack-power lots the venture bids","threshold":"No PO by month 6 post-launch","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"First-year revenue disclosures of comparable rack-power/DC-hardware entrants","where":"Crunchbase/annual filings of recent PDU or rack-infrastructure startups entering EU","threshold":"Comparables clustering below 0.5% of their stated SAM in year 1","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build a bottom-up pipeline model (named accounts × win rate × cycle time × months of selling window) to replace the top-down 1% before fundraising commitments are set against YAM","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Directly replaces the contested benchmark with venture-specific arithmetic"},{"action":"Re-baseline the internal plan on a 24-month obtainable window with quarterly gates, so Year-1 misses don't cascade into a credibility problem with the board","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"firmware-security-audit-gate","title":"The firmware/security audit gate precedes every intelligent-PDU listing — and it isn't in the funnel","narrative":"An intelligent PDU is a networked device sitting on the management LAN of critical infrastructure. Before any large CE operator issues a PO, the vendor must clear a security prequalification that a dumb-metal vendor never faces: vulnerability disclosure process, signed-firmware update infrastructure, SBOM, penetration evidence, increasingly IEC 62443-style artifacts as the EU Cyber Resilience Act obligations phase in. This gate is serial with — not parallel to — CE/TÜV product certification, and it is audited against infrastructure the pre-launch venture has to build during the very year YAM assumes it is harvesting. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut names only the classic cert stack; the security gate is a second, slower door in front of the same customers, and it opens per-operator, not per-product.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor","serviceableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Large-operator prequalification for networked power devices requires an auditable firmware update/PSIRT/SBOM capability before AVL listing; the documented EU entry stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, FCC) is already broader than the ledger's derivation names, and cyber requirements sit on top of it","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c4","ledger:serviceableFactor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"A pre-launch entrant builds this infrastructure and passes per-operator audits inside the same 12 months YAM covers; each audit cycle runs one to two quarters, and the primary-buyer choice (large operators) selects exactly the buyers with the heaviest security gates","refs":["profile:Stage","profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The gate serially delays the first PO (compressing the effective selling window inside obtainableFactor) and forecloses security-gated accounts entirely for Year 1 (a reach shortfall inside serviceableFactor) — neither effect appears in the static 0.55 or the 1%","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","ledger:serviceableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The founder's risk list has a 'certifications' line item, and it looks handled — CE/TÜV are known, scheduled, budgeted. The security audit is invisible from the outside because it is buyer-side process, not published standard: it appears for the first time as a 40-page vendor questionnaire attached to the first serious RFQ, after the model's clock has already started.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Two or more large CE operators (or their integrators) issuing rack-PDU RFQs in the venture's first two quarters with no firmware-security/SBOM prequalification requirement, or confirming that product CE marking alone suffices for AVL listing.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Security prequalification for networked infrastructure devices is standard practice at hyperscale/large-colo buyers and CRA obligations are phasing in, but mid-market and distributor channels gate far less strictly; probability reflects the venture's explicit large-operator-first choice.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.8,"note":"Security-gated large accounts unreachable within Year 1 despite product certification"},{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.5,"note":"First-PO delay of ~2 quarters halves the effective Year-1 selling window"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Vendor-security questionnaire content (SBOM, signed firmware, PSIRT, 62443 mapping) in rack-power prequalification packets","where":"Procurement/supplier portals of Equinix, Digital Realty, NorthC and other CE colo operators; first three RFQs the venture receives","threshold":"≥2 of first 3 RFQs requiring SBOM or firmware-security attestation before quotation","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"CRA horizontal-standard timeline for network-connected industrial products","where":"CEN/CENELEC JTC13 docket and Commission CRA implementing-act publications","threshold":"Harmonized standard citing connected power-distribution devices with obligations landing inside the venture's first 24 months","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run a mock security audit against two target operators' published supplier requirements pre-launch to size the true listing lead time per account","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Converts an unknown serial gate into a scheduled one; de-risks the Year-1 timing assumption"},{"action":"Ship with a third-party-audited firmware signing and PSIRT process at launch (certifiable artifact, not a roadmap slide), and sequence first sales through channels with lighter security gates (distributors, mid-market) while operator audits run","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"framework-agreements-foreclose-large-operator-cell","title":"Global framework agreements make the 0.40 large-operator cell mostly non-contestable in the venture's window","narrative":"The model's biggest customer cell — large operators at 0.40 of PDU spend — encodes the assumption 'large operators buy direct, therefore a direct-selling entrant can compete for them.' But the way large operators buy direct is through multi-year global framework agreements and integrated infrastructure packages with incumbents whose customer lists already span every hyperscaler and major colo. A framework doesn't shrink the cell's spend; it forecloses it: the PDU line is decided once, globally, years at a time, and never comes to regional tender. For a pre-launch regional entrant whose profile explicitly targets large operators first, the nominal 0.40 collapses to the sliver of that spend outside framework coverage — while the model's YAM math quietly credits the whole cell as contestable.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Incumbent rack-power vendors hold entrenched direct relationships with the exact buyers in this cell — Vertiv's disclosed major-customer list alone covers Equinix and the global telcos/hyperscalers — and data-center demand is concentrating supplier order books around these accounts","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c12","digest:cust-mix#c0"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The 0.40 value itself is an unsourced internal estimate: no retrieved source substantiates the channel split, and the one hard number cited (30% of one supplier's orders) measures order composition, not buyer-type contestability — so the cell carries no evidence about how much of it ever reaches open competition","refs":["digest:cust-mix#c0","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"If framework-covered spend is removed, the large-operator cell available to a new regional entrant in its first 12–24 months is roughly half its nominal size, and the venture's chosen go-to-market points at the most-foreclosed cell first","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?"]}],"whyMissable":"'Large operators buy direct' sounds like an access advantage — no distributor margin, a named-account sales motion the founder knows how to run. The foreclosure hides inside the same fact: direct purchasing at scale means framework purchasing, and framework spend never appears as a losable deal, so the pipeline simply looks mysteriously thin rather than visibly blocked.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Three CE large-operator procurement interviews (or won/lost tender records) showing rack-PDU purchases regularly competed line-by-line at regional level outside global frameworks — e.g., annual PDU bake-offs open to non-incumbent vendors.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"Framework and integrated-package procurement by large operators is documented behavior in the digest corpus and incumbent customer lists overlap the target accounts almost completely, but the actual framework-coverage share of PDU spend is unquantified — the cell value itself is admitted guesswork in both directions.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"set","value":0.2,"note":"Roughly half of nominal large-operator PDU spend sits under multi-year frameworks/integrated packages and is non-contestable in the venture's window"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"New multi-year power-infrastructure framework or integrated-rack supply announcements naming CE operators","where":"Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton quarterly earnings calls and press rooms; operator capex-day presentations","threshold":"≥1 new CE-relevant operator signing a global power framework covering rack distribution","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Standalone rack-PDU lots appearing (or not) in operator tenders, and their bidder counts","where":"TED (EU tender portal) and operator supplier portals for DE/NL colo fit-outs","threshold":"Standalone PDU lots absent, sole-sourced, or drawing ≤2 bidders across two consecutive quarters","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission the three CE operator/distributor procurement interviews already named in the ledger's promotionPath, specifically instrumented to measure framework coverage of PDU spend","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Replaces the unsourced 0.40 with a contestability-adjusted split"},{"action":"Re-sequence go-to-market to lead with colocation mid-market and OEM/integrator channels where line-item bake-offs still occur, holding large operators as a Year-2+ motion entered via a framework-holder partnership","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"prelet-pipeline-lockout","title":"The growth the CAGR promises was electrically specified before launch day — Year 1 can only sell into the installed base","narrative":"Nine percent CAGR reads as a tailwind: new halls, new racks, new PDU line items every quarter of Year 1. But new-build DC electrical procurement is locked at design freeze, and with long-lead power equipment (transformers, switchgear) now booked years out, operators are locking entire electrical BOMs — PDUs riding along — far earlier than the 12–24-month window the ledger's own crossCheck uses to justify its ceiling. Every megawatt that energizes during the venture's first 12 months had its rack-power vendor chosen while the venture was still pre-launch. That leaves Year-1 demand confined to retrofit, refresh and expansion churn in the installed base — a materially smaller and slower pool than 'the market' — while the model applies its 1% to the whole SAM as if new-build spend were reachable on the venture's clock.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"New-build electrical BOMs for capacity energizing in the venture's Year 1 were specified at design freeze, before the venture existed as a qualified vendor; the ledger's own crossCheck concedes gear is 'locked 12–24mo before fit-out' and current long-lead conditions push lock-in earlier still","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor","profile:Stage"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The market's growth is concentrated in exactly this pre-committed new-build pipeline (the substance of the 9% CAGR), so the contestable Year-1 pool is the installed base's refresh cycle — and what standalone PDU procurement evidence exists shows small, sometimes sole-sourced lots with 1–2 bidders, not open bake-offs","refs":["ledger:shape.cagr","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c12"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Generic obtainable-share benchmarks (1% Year 1) assume the full SAM is time-reachable; no DC-hardware-specific benchmark exists that discounts for procurement lock-in, so the model has no mechanism to encode it","refs":["digest:obtainable-benchmark#c0","digest:obtainable-benchmark#c2"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor applied to the full SAM double-counts pre-committed new-build spend as reachable; the truly contestable Year-1 share sits at or below the band floor","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The founder's plan treats the CAGR as an accelerant and 1% as conservative precisely because the market is growing — 'we only need a tiny slice of a rising tide.' The trap is that the rising portion of the tide is the least reachable portion on a 12-month clock; the model has no node that separates committed-pipeline demand from contestable demand, so nothing in the ledger flags it.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Documented cases of CE new-build data centers running competitive rack-PDU selection within 6 months of fit-out (i.e., inside the venture's selling window), or an operator confirming PDUs are procured late as a commodity line decoupled from the electrical design freeze.","likelihood":{"value":0.5,"rationale":"Early electrical lock-in for new builds is well-documented industry behavior and the ledger's own crossCheck relies on a 12–24-month version of it; the offsetting uncertainty is how much PDU spend is genuinely late-cycle commodity purchasing versus BOM-committed — unquantified in every retrieved source.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.005,"note":"Year-1 capture limited to installed-base retrofit/refresh; new-build pipeline pre-committed before launch"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Stage at which rack-PDU vendor is fixed in CE fit-out tenders (bundled at design freeze vs late separate lot)","where":"TED tender documents for DE/NL data-center fit-outs; fit-out contractors' bid packages","threshold":"PDU line bundled into general-electrical packages in ≥3 of 4 sampled tenders","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Pre-let percentages and long-lead procurement commentary for CE capacity additions","where":"Operator and hyperscaler capex calls (Equinix, Digital Realty quarterly); Vertiv/Schneider backlog commentary","threshold":"Pre-let >80% with electrical packages cited as booked >18 months out","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Map the 2026–27 CE new-build pipeline by procurement stage (design freeze reached? electrical package awarded?) to quantify the actually-contestable Year-1 pool","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Separates committed from contestable SAM — the distinction the funnel lacks"},{"action":"Target the installed-base refresh cycle explicitly: build the launch offer around drop-in replacement and retrofit economics (metering upgrades, DCIM integration) rather than new-build spec wins","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":null}]},{"id":"mid-eichrecht-metering-foreclosure","title":"MID/Eichrecht-grade metering gates the billing use case in Germany — half the geography, the core intelligent-PDU value prop","narrative":"The intelligent PDU's premium over dumb power strips rests substantially on outlet- and rack-level metering. But in Germany — 50% of the modeled geography — any meter whose readings are used to bill sub-tenants per measured kWh falls under legal-metrology obligations (MessEG/MID): the metering chain must be conformity-assessed, not merely accurate. A pre-launch entrant whose PDU metering is 'billing-grade' in spec but not MID-certified in law cannot sell the billing use case to German colos at all, and MID module certification is its own multi-quarter, notified-body process that is nowhere on the launch-critical path. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 bundles 'EU cert stack' into one qualitative haircut and never distinguishes this instrument — yet it forecloses the highest-value slice of demand in the largest country cell.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["serviceableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"German legal metrology (MessEG, implementing MID 2014/32/EU) requires conformity-assessed meters wherever measured kWh is billed to third parties — a distinct instrument absent from the ledger's named cert set, which the digest already shows is narrower than the real EU entry stack","refs":["digest:serviceable-benchmark#c13","ledger:serviceableFactor"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Colocation operators re-billing power to tenants are precisely the buyers who pay the intelligent-PDU premium for metering; Germany is 0.50 of the geography split and colocation plus hyperscale wholesale re-billing spans the majority of the segment mix","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","ledger:seg.colocation","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c4"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"MID module B+D certification via a notified body is a multi-quarter engineering-plus-audit effort; for a pre-launch venture it cannot complete inside the same Year 1 the funnel is harvesting, so the German billing-metering demand is foreclosed for the period, not merely delayed","refs":["profile:Stage","digest:serviceable-benchmark#c8"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"serviceableFactor 0.55 silently includes billing-metering demand it cannot legally reach in DE during Year 1; the reachable share sits at the band floor or below until MID certification lands","refs":["ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:geo.DE"]}],"whyMissable":"The product meets or beats billing-class accuracy on its datasheet, so 'metering compliance' looks like a solved engineering problem. Legal metrology is a conformity regime, not an accuracy bar — a distinction invisible until a German colo's procurement lawyer asks for the MID declaration, by which point the tender is lost. And because much colo billing today is capacity-tier rather than per-kWh, early conversations can pass without the gate ever surfacing.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Confirmation from two German colocation operators (or the responsible eichamt/PTB guidance) that PDU-level metering used for tenant re-billing is exempt from MessEG conformity requirements in their billing model, or that estimate/capacity-tier billing makes MID-certified PDU metering commercially irrelevant to their purchase decision.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"The legal obligation for billed per-kWh metering is clear, but its bite depends on how much CE colo billing is actually measured-kWh at PDU level versus capacity-tier or upstream branch-circuit metering — heterogeneity the corpus documents but does not quantify.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"serviceableFactor","op":"set","value":0.45,"note":"German billing-metering demand foreclosed until MID conformity; reachable share drops to band floor"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Billing basis in German colo contract templates and tenders (measured per-kWh at rack vs fixed-kW capacity tiers)","where":"German colo operators' published service schedules; DE colo lots on TED and operator supplier portals","threshold":"Per-kWh sub-metered billing specified in ≥30% of sampled DE colo offerings","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"MID/Eichrecht enforcement posture and guidance on data-center sub-metering","where":"PTB publications and Länder eichamt enforcement notices; VfEB (Eichrecht compliance association) docket","threshold":"Any published guidance or enforcement action explicitly covering DC tenant re-billing meters","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Obtain a legal-metrology opinion plus two German colo billing-practice interviews to determine what fraction of DE PDU demand actually requires MID-conformant metering","type":"information","voiNodeId":"serviceableFactor","note":"Converts the bundled 0.55 haircut into an instrument-by-instrument reach model for the largest country cell"},{"action":"Start MID module certification for the metering subsystem pre-launch in parallel with CE/TÜV, and ship a non-billing SKU positioning (monitoring/capacity-planning) for DE accounts until it lands","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Decouples first revenue from the slowest conformity gate"}]}]}
